1: gôrāl (lot) selects one in ten for Jerusalem. ʿîr haqqōḏeš — “the holy city” — appears only here and in v.18 in Nehemiah; the designation elevates Jerusalem from a ruined administrative center to a sacred space whose re-population is itself a religious obligation. The lottery distributes the burden of urban resettlement equitably; the city needs residents before it can function as the community’s center.
2: hitnaddeḇ (to volunteer willingly) — the Hithpael of nāḏaḇ, meaning to offer oneself voluntarily; the same root as nᵉḏāḇāh (freewill offering). The community’s public praise of these volunteers signals the social honor attached to accepting the hardship of Jerusalem’s sparse urban life; moving to the city was a sacrifice, and the community registers it as such.
3: The genealogical register organizes Jerusalem’s inhabitants by institutional category: tribal (Judah, Benjamin), priestly, Levitical, temple servants. This five-category structure reproduces the pre-exilic temple-city administration — the restored community consciously reconstitutes the Davidic-Solomonic order rather than innovating a new structure.
4: Athaiah of the Perez line of Judah — Perez (pereṣ, “breach”) was the son of Judah and Tamar whose unexpected first birth (Gen 38:29) established the dominant Judahite sub-clan. The Perez genealogy recurs as a mark of Davidic legitimacy (Ruth 4:18-22; Matt 1:3).
5: Maaseiah of the Shilonite line — Šîlōnî may derive from Shiloh (the pre-Jerusalem sanctuary) or from Shelah, Judah’s third son; either way it is a minority Judahite lineage recorded alongside the dominant Perez clan, signaling that the restored city population preserves the full genealogical breadth of the tribe.
6: ʾanšê ḥayil — “capable men” or “men of valor”; 468 from the Perez line alone. ḥayil spans military prowess, social capability, and economic substance — the same term used for the “woman of valor” in Prov 31:10. Its use here marks the Jerusalem settlers as a capable, capable class, not a residual remnant.
7: Sallu of the Meshullam line from Benjamin — the genealogy traces back six generations, anchoring the Benjaminite settlers within their known pre-exilic clan structures. The depth of the genealogy signals legitimate continuity with the pre-exilic community.
8: Gabbai and Sallai add to the Benjaminite total: 928 settlers from Benjamin in Jerusalem. The tribe of Benjamin’s significant representation reflects that Jerusalem fell within Benjamin’s tribal territory in the original allocation (Josh 18:28); their presence in the city has both genealogical and territorial legitimacy.
9: pāqîḏ (overseer) and mišneh (second-in-command) — the dual-leadership structure mirrors the city’s administrative organization; Joel oversees the Benjaminites while Judah son of Senuah is second over the city as a whole. The term mišneh (second) is the same used for the “second quarter” of Jerusalem (Zeph 1:10) and for the second-in-command to the king (2 Chr 28:7).
10: Jedaiah son of Joiarib and Jachin — two priestly family heads whose names recur throughout the restoration records. Joiarib’s family is listed first (also in 1 Chr 24:7) and will later produce the Maccabean priests; Jachin was one of the two pillars of Solomon’s temple (1 Kgs 7:21).
11: Seraiah as śar bêṯ-hāʾĕlōhîm — “officer/prince of the house of God” — the chief administrative officer of the temple; the term śar indicates a position of governing authority. The genealogy traces through Zadok, establishing that the chief priest of the restored temple continues the Zadokite line that Ezekiel’s temple vision required (Ezek 44:15).
12: 822 relatives serving in the temple — the largest priestly contingent listed. Their service is described as ʿōśê hamᵉlāʾḵāh labayiṯ (“doing the work of the house”) — the same term for the temple work that David organized and Solomon executed; the verb-noun pair carries continuity with the pre-exilic cult.
13: Adaiah leads 242 heads of ancestral houses — rāʾšê ʾāḇôṯ (“heads of fathers’ houses”) is the administrative term for family-clan leaders who bear responsibility for the entire extended household. The relatively small number (242) compared to the total suggests these are senior representatives, not the full priestly count.
14: 128 ʾanšê ḥayil under Zabdiel — the “men of valor” designation applied to priests signals a martial-administrative quality alongside the cultic function; priests in the post-exilic community sometimes combined cultic and civic leadership. The name Zabdiel means “gift of God” (zeḇaḏ ʾēl).
15: The Levitical register begins with Shemaiah, tracing back to Bunni — one of the Levites who sealed the covenant in Neh 10:9. The depth of Levitical genealogies varies: some trace four-five generations, others only two, reflecting varying states of record-preservation after the exile.
16: Shabbethai and Jozabad are over hamᵉlāʾkāh haḥîṣônāh — “the external work/affairs of the house of God”; this likely covers administration, procurement, and oversight functions outside the sanctuary itself, as distinct from the cultic Levitical duties performed within. The division between internal sanctuary service and external administrative work mirrors the Chronicler’s organization of Levitical duties (1 Chr 23).
17: Mattaniah “who directed the hôḏāʾāh in prayer” — hôḏāʾāh is the noun from yāḏāh (to give thanks, to praise); Mattaniah leads the community’s formal thanksgiving liturgy, an Asaphite function (his line traces to Asaph, David’s chief musician); Bakbukiah is his counterpart leading the antiphonal response. The thanking/praising function is the Levitical role most directly continued into NT worship (Col 3:16).
18: 284 Levites total in Jerusalem — a relatively small number for a city that requires their full temple ministry. The scarcity of Levites willing to return from Babylon was a recurring problem (Ezra 8:15-20 records Ezra having to specifically recruit Levites who had not come); those listed here represent the committed core.
19: 172 gatekeepers — šōʿᵃrîm (those who guard the gates). The gatekeepers’ function was both cultic (guarding the temple gates) and civic (controlling access to the city); the restoration of this role is part of the normalization of Jerusalem as a functioning city-sanctuary after the wall’s completion.
20: The priests and Levites outside Jerusalem lived in their ancestral towns — naḥᵃlāṯô (his inheritance); the ancestral land-holding system is preserved even for those serving in the temple. The Levitical towns assigned in Joshua 21 are the legal basis for this dispersal, though many towns had been redistributed or occupied during the exile.
21: The nᵉṯînîm (temple servants) on the Ophel — the hill south of the temple mount. The nᵉṯînîm were subordinate temple workers, perhaps descendants of prisoners of war given (nāṯan = given) to temple service; their placement on the Ophel rather than within the city walls proper reflects their lower social status within the cultic hierarchy.
22: Uzzi the overseer of Levites in Jerusalem — traced as an Asaphite, the family of David’s chief musician. The Asaphites’ prominence in both the pre-exilic and post-exilic periods signals the continuity of David’s musical institution; the restored temple’s musical life is not an innovation but a recovery.
23: dāḇar hammelek — “the king’s decree/command”; the Persian royal court provided a fixed daily portion (ḥōq-yôm bᵉyômô) for the singers, subsidizing the temple music program. This is the same Persian administrative generosity that funded the temple rebuilding (Ezra 6:8-10); the empire’s patronage of the temple musicians reflects the Persian policy of supporting local cultic practices as a tool of imperial stability.
24: Pethahiah as yāḏ lammelek — literally “hand to the king” — the Persian royal liaison for community affairs; he is from the Zerah line of Judah (the other main Judahite clan after Perez). This liaison role represents the Jewish community’s formal interface with the Persian administration, a position requiring both cultural fluency and community trust.
25: The village settlements of Judah begin with Kiriath-arba (the ancient name for Hebron, Gen 23:2) — a city with deep patriarchal associations (Abraham purchased land there). Mentioning Kiriath-arba rather than Hebron may be a deliberate archaism that claims the oldest layer of Israelite territorial presence.
26: Jeshua and Moladah are towns in the Negev region. Moladah (“birth”) was listed in Josh 15:26 as a Judahite town and in Josh 19:2 as Simeonite — the overlapping claims reflect the fluid tribal boundaries of the far south. The restoration community is reoccupying the full territorial extent of pre-exilic Judah, not just the Jerusalem core.
27: Beersheba — bᵉʾēr šeḇaʿ (well of seven/oath) — the southern boundary marker of Israel in the common phrase “from Dan to Beersheba.” Its inclusion in the resettlement signals the community’s ambition to restore the full extent of historical Judah, including the peripheral southern region that was the least likely to have survived the Babylonian deportation with any continuity.
28: Ziklag is David’s Philistine base of operations before becoming king (1 Sam 27:6); its inclusion among the resettled towns claims the full western territory associated with the Davidic kingdom. The list moves from the Negev (Beersheba) across to former Philistine borderland.
29: En-rimmon, Zorah, and Jarmuth — En-rimmon in the Negev; Zorah is Samson’s home territory (Judg 13:2); Jarmuth is listed as a royal Canaanite city defeated by Joshua (Josh 10:3). The recitation of these towns implicitly reclaims the entire pre-Babylonian territorial inheritance.
30: The settlement range: “from Beersheba all the way to the Valley of Hinnom” — the southern extent to the valley just outside Jerusalem’s south wall. Gê-hinnom (Valley of Hinnom) was the site of Molech worship that Josiah defiled (2 Kgs 23:10); its presence on the southern boundary is a reminder of the cult-sites the restoration must leave purified.
31: The Benjaminite settlements begin at Geba — Geḇaʿ (hill), a Levitical town north of Jerusalem. Michmash was the site of Jonathan’s famous single-handed victory (1 Sam 14); Bethel, though long associated with Jeroboam’s rival worship, is reclaimed as a Benjaminite settlement site.
32: Anathoth — the Levitical city of Jeremiah’s origin (Jer 1:1); its inclusion in the resettlement list means the community settling Anathoth is returning to the city whose son had warned most urgently about the exile they have just survived. Nob was the priestly city massacred by Saul (1 Sam 22:19).
33: Hazor and Ramah — Ramah was the site of Samuel’s home, school, and burial (1 Sam 7:17; 25:1); Gittaim appears in 2 Sam 4:3 as the refuge of Beerothites who fled during the civil war period. The Benjaminite resettlement names echo the entire narrative range of the pre-exilic period.
34: Hadid and Zeboim — Hadid reappears in the return lists (Ezra 2:33; Neh 7:37); Zeboim means “hyenas” — a topographic name for a rugged ravine area northeast of Jerusalem. These are smaller settlements filling out the Benjaminite territory north of the city.
35: Lod and Ono in the “Valley of Craftsmen” (gê-hāḥarāšîm) — this valley, where artisans had apparently concentrated, is the only explicitly occupational place-name in the list. 1 Chr 4:14 attributes the founding of this “valley of craftsmen” to a descendant of Judah (Joab), though it falls in Benjaminite territory here.
36: Some Levitical divisions from Judah were assigned to Benjamin — the Levites did not hold tribal territory but were distributed across both tribes’ settlements. This final note emphasizes that the Levitical presence extends the religious infrastructure across the full settlement area, not just in Jerusalem proper.
